headline: Thirteen colonies declare a republic while Qianlong's China compiles its imperial library
summary: On 4 July 1776 the Continental Congress in Philadelphia adopted the Declaration of Independence, launching a republic that would in time reorder Atlantic politics. In the same year the Scottish moral philosopher Adam Smith published the Wealth of Nations; Captain Cook sailed from Plymouth on the third voyage that would open Hawaii to European eyes; a Spanish presidio was founded at San Francisco Bay; Warren Hastings governed a British East India Company that had taken over Bengal's revenues; the Qianlong emperor's Four Treasuries compilation was gathering an empire's books at Beijing; and the transatlantic slave trade was near its terrible peak, moving nearly a hundred thousand Africans a year to the Americas.

snapshots.text: In Philadelphia on 2 July 1776 the Second Continental Congress voted for independence on the Lee Resolution and, two days later, on 4 July, adopted the Declaration of Independence drafted principally by Thomas Jefferson of Virginia and revised by Benjamin Franklin, John Adams, and the drafting committee. The document was printed as a broadside by John Dunlap that night and read publicly in Philadelphia on 8 July. Militarily the year was disastrous for the Continental cause: Washington's army was pushed off Long Island by General William Howe on 27 August, forced from Manhattan on 15 September, and driven across New Jersey by December; the war was rescued only by the Christmas-night crossing of the Delaware and the surprise victory at Trenton on 26 December. In the west, Cherokee attacks on frontier settlements were beaten off. On the Pacific coast the Spanish presidio at San Francisco was established under Juan Bautista de Anza on 17 September and Mission San Francisco de Asís was founded shortly after; Franciscan missions were expanding across Alta California. On 1 August the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata was formally created at Buenos Aires, detaching the vast South Atlantic hinterland from Lima. In Portuguese Brazil the marquis of Pombal's reforms were near their end (he would fall in 1777); silver from Mexico and Peru remained the fiscal foundation of the Bourbon empire. Everywhere in the Americas, Indigenous nations — Haudenosaunee, Comanche, Ohlone, Maori, Mapuche — navigated the pressures of expanding settler and imperial states.

European politics in 1776 were shadowed by the American war and by intellectual ferment. On 9 March the London publisher William Strahan and Thomas Cadell issued the first edition of Adam Smith's An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations, an intellectual event whose reception among ministers and merchants would slowly transform political economy. Earlier the same year, on 17 February, Edward Gibbon had published the first volume of The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire. Jeremy Bentham published his Fragment on Government. In France, Louis XVI's radical controller-general Anne-Robert-Jacques Turgot was dismissed on 12 May 1776 after his physiocratic reforms alienated the parlements and courtiers; Jacques Necker soon took charge of finances and turned to international borrowing to fund covert aid to the American rebels, which Pierre Beaumarchais had begun channelling through the shell firm Rodrigue Hortalez et Cie. In St Petersburg Catherine II ('the Great') presided over the aftermath of the Pugachev rebellion (crushed 1774) and the first partition of Poland-Lithuania (1772); the Peace of Küçük Kaynarca of 1774 was reordering the Black Sea. In Vienna the co-regent Joseph II, son of Empress Maria Theresa, was preparing the Josephine reforms. In Ingolstadt in Bavaria, Adam Weishaupt founded the Order of the Illuminati on 1 May. In Rome Pope Pius VI reigned; the papal Inquisition was moribund but the abolition of the Jesuits (1773) still convulsed Catholic Europe.

The Qing empire under the Qianlong Emperor (r. 1735-1796), grandson of Kangxi, was at its greatest territorial extent and demographic peak, ruling a population now above three hundred million from the Forbidden City in Beijing. The second Jinchuan war in eastern Tibet, one of the costliest of the emperor's Ten Great Campaigns, had ended the previous year; the Xinjiang conquests of 1755-1759 that destroyed the Dzungar Khanate had folded Central Asia into the empire. In 1772 the emperor had ordered the compilation of the Siku Quanshu (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries), an imperial encyclopedia of Chinese thought; by 1776 the compilation office in Beijing under Ji Yun and Lu Xixiong was screening thousands of works, copying about 3,450 titles into 36,000 volumes with fifteen thousand copyists — while simultaneously banning or burning some 3,100 works judged seditious to Manchu rule. The favourite Heshen was rising at court; corruption and military overreach were beginning to strain the treasury. In Japan, the Tokugawa shogun Ieharu (r. 1760-1786) governed under the influence of the reforming and later disgraced chief senior councillor Tanuma Okitsugu; Emperor Go-Momozono reigned in Kyoto. In Korea King Jeongjo (Yi San, r. 1776-1800) ascended the throne of Joseon on the deposition of his grandfather's regency, one of the great scholarly monarchs of Korean history. In Đại Việt the ruling Trịnh lords in the north and the Nguyễn lords in the south were about to be shattered by the Tây Sơn rebellion (from 1771).

The Indian subcontinent in 1776 was in the process of falling piecemeal to the East India Company. Warren Hastings, appointed Governor of Bengal in 1772 and made Governor-General under the Regulating Act of 1773, ruled from Calcutta with a fractious Supreme Council of four in which he was frequently outvoted by Philip Francis and others. The Company held Bengal's diwani (revenue collection) under a fig-leaf grant from the shadow Mughal emperor Shah Alam II, whose court at Delhi was under Maratha protection since 1772. On 1 March 1776 the Treaty of Purandar with the Peshwa Madhavrao II's ministers ended, on paper, the First Anglo-Maratha War (the fighting continued to 1782). Nana Fadnavis and Mahadji Scindia dominated Maratha politics from Poona. In Awadh the Nawab Asaf-ud-Daula (from 1775) ruled a Company client-state from a new capital at Lucknow. In Mysore, Haidar Ali continued the modernised military state that would fight three Anglo-Mysore wars; his son Tipu Sultan was a rising commander. In the Punjab, the Sikh misls governed a patchwork of jagirs. In Afghanistan the Durrani empire under Timur Shah (from 1772) was in slow decline after Ahmad Shah. The Bengal famine of 1770 that killed perhaps a third of the province still shadowed Hastings's administration; he had built granaries and codified Hindu and Muslim law digests as part of the Company's transformation into a de facto Indian state. In Nepal Prithvi Narayan Shah's Gorkha unification (completed 1769) was still fresh; the Qing invasion of Tibet in response to Gorkha raids lay ahead in 1788-1793.

The Ottoman Empire in 1776 was ruled by Sultan Abdul Hamid I (r. 1774-1789), who had inherited from his brother Mustafa III the disastrous fallout of the Russo-Turkish War of 1768-1774: the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca of July 1774 had ceded the northern Black Sea coast, granted Russia navigation rights through the Straits, made the Crimean Khanate nominally independent (Russia would annex it in 1783), and given the Russian tsar a claim to protect Ottoman Orthodox Christians that would poison relations for a century. The empire in 1776 was consolidating internally: Baron de Tott, a French artillery expert, had recently reorganised Ottoman fortifications and gunnery. In Egypt the Mamluk beys under the strongman Muhammad Bey Abu al-Dhahab ruled semi-independently of Istanbul until his death in 1775; Ibrahim Bey and Murad Bey now held Cairo. In Iraq the local Mamluk pashas of Baghdad ruled autonomously. In Iran the Zand dynasty founder Karim Khan Zand (r. 1751-1779), one of the most humane rulers of Persian history, governed a peaceable state from Shiraz, whose lovely gardens and buildings he patronised; his death in 1779 would open the country to the brutal civil war from which the Qajar dynasty would emerge. In Arabia the Saudi-Wahhabi first emirate under Muhammad ibn Saud's son Abdulaziz had spread the Wahhabi movement across most of Najd from its capital at Diriyah, threatening Ottoman and sharifian authority in the Hejaz. In Morocco the Alaouite sultan Muhammad III (r. 1757-1790) ruled a state that had, in 1777, become the first to recognise American independence.

West Africa in 1776 was traversed by the Atlantic slave trade at close to its historical peak: the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database records approximately eighty to ninety thousand enslaved Africans shipped annually across the Atlantic in the 1770s. The Asante empire in the Gold Coast forest zone, under the Asantehene Osei Kwadwo (r. 1764-1777) from Kumasi, controlled a centralised state built on gold and on trade with Europeans on the coast, and had recently reformed its bureaucracy toward merit-based appointment of central officials. To the east the Kingdom of Dahomey under King Kpengla (r. 1774-1789) ruled from Abomey, an important slave-trading state whose Amazons (Mino) women warriors and annual Xwetanu ceremonies were becoming known to European visitors. Further east the Oyo empire of the Yoruba dominated much of the interior of modern south-western Nigeria, with the Alaafin ruling from Oyo-Ile; Oyo had recently defeated Dahomey and made it tributary. In the western Sudan the Bambara kingdoms of Segou and Kaarta continued; further north, Kanem-Bornu was in decline. In the Sudanic east the Funj sultanate at Sennar ruled the Blue Nile country. In the Ethiopian highlands the Zemene Mesafint (Era of the Princes) had begun in 1769, in which the Solomonic emperors at Gondar were ceremonial figureheads and provincial warlords held real power. On the Swahili coast the Sultanate of Oman, having driven out the Portuguese in the seventeenth century, was consolidating its Zanzibar-Mombasa-Kilwa network. At the Cape, the Dutch VOC colony under Governor Joachim van Plettenberg pushed the frontier eastward against Khoekhoe and San communities; the first Boer-Xhosa conflicts on the Great Fish River were imminent.

On 12 July 1776 Captain James Cook sailed from Plymouth on his third and final Pacific voyage in HMS Resolution, joined by Charles Clerke in HMS Discovery. The nominal purpose was to return the Society Islander Omai (Mai) to his home; the true objective was to seek a Northwest Passage from the Pacific side. Cook would touch at the Cape, Kerguelen, and Tasmania, reach New Zealand's Queen Charlotte Sound in February 1777, spend most of 1777 in the Society Islands and the newly explored Cook Islands, and then in January 1778 make the first documented European contact with the Hawaiian archipelago (which he named the Sandwich Islands) at Kauai and Niihau. He would die at Kealakekua Bay on 14 February 1779 in an escalating dispute over a stolen cutter. The voyage would open Hawaii to European trade and, over the following decades, to devastating epidemic disease. In Aotearoa (New Zealand) Māori iwi continued the world of pā fortifications, kūmara horticulture, and elaborate rangatiratanga chieftaincy that Cook and Banks had first described on the Endeavour voyage of 1769-1770. In Australia the eastern coast had been claimed by Britain in 1770 but not yet settled — the First Fleet would arrive at Botany Bay only in 1788; hundreds of Aboriginal nations continued their tens-of-thousands-of-years-old traditions of land management, songline networks, and long-distance exchange. On the northern coast the seasonal Makassan trepang trade from Sulawesi continued. Across Micronesia, Guam remained the sole Spanish colony (from 1668); the rest of the Pacific was self-governing.
